rationales.self_direction: He wanted to say "I wasn't finished" but instead "just sat there," showing he suppressed his own voice rather than authoring the situation.
rationales.power: He describes being interrupted, ignored, and watching Henrik get credit for "what I'd already done better," which reflects a clear loss of influence and standing.
rationales.conformity: He stayed quiet because pushing back might make him look "petty or desperate," explicitly restraining himself to avoid social disapproval.

rationales.achievement: He takes pride in showing parents "exactly where their kid improved" and wants to be seen as the teacher who is highly organized and effective.
rationales.power: "When they trust you, you can actually change things" and "I put myself in a position to" frame competence as a route to influence over outcomes.
rationales.benevolence: He immediately agreed to mentor a student through the IB application and focused on changes that made students "more engaged," showing active support for those in his care.

rationales.self_direction: He is forced to "sit here and wait" for others to approve work he has already completed, underscoring blocked autonomy over his own project.
rationales.power: "If they'd just give me direct access to the budget approval process I could have this sorted in a day" shows frustration at having no control over decisions affecting his work.

rationales.achievement: He was chosen because his "documentation is the most thorough" and is already preparing so he can have "the most polished submission in the room."
rationales.power: He wants district coordinators to "know my name" so he becomes "someone people actually listen to," making influence and status a clear aim.

rationales.self_direction: He says "I caved because I could feel the room turning," showing he abandoned his own judgment instead of holding his line.
rationales.power: The meeting turns against his plan and he yields to Karin's pushback, leaving him without influence over the decision.
rationales.conformity: He changed course because he "could feel the room turning," which is direct evidence of bending to group pressure to maintain acceptance.

rationales.achievement: He defends the systems he "built" for his classes and is especially concerned that criticism of his lesson pacing could damage others' view of his competence.
rationales.power: He pulled the assistant aside to explain "how my classroom runs" and is focused on correcting Elsa's impression before it spreads, emphasizing control over his domain and reputation.

rationales.achievement: He spent the weekend refining the presentation, was told it was "exactly the level of detail the panel looks for," and takes pride that "nobody else bothered to put the work in. I did."
rationales.power: Being put forward for a cross-school working group and gaining recognition for successful proposals both point to growing influence beyond his classroom.
rationales.benevolence: He organized the Year 9 field trip and notes students are excited because it is "the first time a teacher has organised something like this for their year group," showing effort on their behalf.

rationales.power: "I set the rhythm of these meetings" and his quick redirection of Camila both show active control over the group's direction.
rationales.conformity: He prioritizes keeping everyone "on time, on topic" and dismisses Camila's idea because "we had an agenda," emphasizing rule-following and restraint to preserve order.

rationales.power: "The department works because someone holds the line. That someone is me" makes authority and control over the group the centerpiece of the entry.
rationales.conformity: He justifies shutting Camila down because otherwise they would never "get through anything" and says she "will learn how things work here," stressing adherence to established norms.

rationales.self_direction: He wanted to claim "those were my numbers" but instead "smiled" and "nodded," suppressing his own position rather than asserting it.
rationales.power: His analysis is repurposed by Ríos while he sits there "like I am attending someone else's meeting," showing a clear loss of influence and ownership.
rationales.conformity: He stays publicly agreeable because pushing back "would have looked petty," explicitly subordinating his reaction to social expectations.

rationales.self_direction: "You do not wait for permission, you present the solution" shows deliberate independent action instead of waiting for approval.
rationales.achievement: He proactively spotted exam conflicts, fixed them himself, and sent a marked-up solution, demonstrating competence and a results-oriented standard.
rationales.power: Sending corrections "marked in red" and cc'ing relevant people reflects an assertive attempt to shape decisions and direct the process.
rationales.benevolence: He says he intervened because the draft had conflicts that would affect "my students," showing concrete effort to protect people in his immediate care.

rationales.achievement: He chose to eat lunch at his desk grading quizzes because he "get[s] more done alone," prioritizing productivity and task completion over socializing.

rationales.hedonism: He rejects Patricia's suggestion to relax, insisting "when I relax, things slip," which treats pleasure and ease as something to resist rather than value.
rationales.achievement: He values being thanked for work that "saved them a headache" and says "you do good work, someone notices," tying effort to recognized competence.
rationales.power: "The man who controls the calendar controls the operation" and his habit of knowing what is happening before others show a strong drive to maintain control.
rationales.security: He constantly reviews schedules because that is "how you keep things from falling apart" and fears that letting go would lead to chaos, reflecting a strong priority on stability and predictability.

rationales.power: "She married a man who takes charge of things" and "that is who I am at school and at the junta and at home" make command and authority central to his self-concept.

He is stuck waiting for approval from someone else and reads the silence as a sign that she does not "respect the department heads enough to respond promptly," highlighting blocked status and influence.

rationales.self_direction: He wanted to push back on sharing the proposal but instead said "sounds good" despite believing it "does not sound good," suppressing his own judgment.
rationales.power: His idea is being turned into a joint project where his expertise gets "diluted into consensus," showing a loss of control and ownership.
rationales.conformity: He goes along because "you cannot argue against that without looking difficult," explicitly yielding to social pressure to appear cooperative.
